title: Scheduler-Attention Handshake Regression — Closure & Preventive Action Plan
preview: Summary: On 2026-03-11 we observed a sustained P95 token generation latency regression (~1.8x) for a subset of medium-length chat workloads. The regression correlated with a rollout of a scheduler change that altered when attention-tiling hints are passed to the kernel selector. Root cause: a timing/handshake mismatch where the scheduler occasionally forwarded requests before the attention tiling metadata was populated, causing the kernel selector to choose a conservative dense-attention kernel rather than the optimized tiled kernel. This increased compute and memory pressure leading to longer queues and higher P95. Scope: ~4% of overall host traffic, concentrated on models with sequence lengths 512-1024 and batching enabled. Immediate impact: customer-facing latency spikes, a handful of customer support tickets, and automated SLA margin alerts. Goal: close the incident with targeted fixes, verify rollbacks and roll-forward safety, and implement guards to prevent recurrence.
text: Summary: On 2026-03-11 we observed a sustained P95 token generation latency regression (~1.8x) for a subset of medium-length chat workloads. The regression correlated with a rollout of a scheduler change that altered when attention-tiling hints are passed to the kernel selector. Root cause: a timing/handshake mismatch where the scheduler occasionally forwarded requests before the attention tiling metadata was populated, causing the kernel selector to choose a conservative dense-attention kernel rather than the optimized tiled kernel. This increased compute and memory pressure leading to longer queues and higher P95. Scope: ~4% of overall host traffic, concentrated on models with sequence lengths 512-1024 and batching enabled. Immediate impact: customer-facing latency spikes, a handful of customer support tickets, and automated SLA margin alerts. Goal: close the incident with targeted fixes, verify rollbacks and roll-forward safety, and implement guards to prevent recurrence.

title: Token Line-Item Orbit: role-aware cost builder & CSV export UX
preview: Design a compact, role-sensitive token-level cost analytics canvas ("Orbit") that surfaces line-item cost breakdowns, attribution, and an export builder for CSVs and billing feeds. The component must scale from 10s to 100k line-items via progressive disclosure, support role-scoped views (engineer, finance, product), and provide a pricing-pattern preview (rate bands, SKU mapping) before export.
text: Design a compact, role-sensitive token-level cost analytics canvas ("Orbit") that surfaces line-item cost breakdowns, attribution, and an export builder for CSVs and billing feeds. The component must scale from 10s to 100k line-items via progressive disclosure, support role-scoped views (engineer, finance, product), and provide a pricing-pattern preview (rate bands, SKU mapping) before export.

title: Micro-Inference Slice Profiler for Tokenized Latency & Cache Attribution
preview: Introduce a lightweight, low-overhead profiler that emits fine-grained "token slice" events from the serving runtime. Each token-slice event will capture sequential micro-steps (prefix lookup, KV fetch, kernel compute, attention update, serialization) and a cache-hit flag so we can attribute latency and unit cost impact to cache behavior. The goal is a unified signal for performance analysis, regression detection, and cost-accuracy tradeoff experiments across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.
text: Introduce a lightweight, low-overhead profiler that emits fine-grained "token slice" events from the serving runtime. Each token-slice event will capture sequential micro-steps (prefix lookup, KV fetch, kernel compute, attention update, serialization) and a cache-hit flag so we can attribute latency and unit cost impact to cache behavior. The goal is a unified signal for performance analysis, regression detection, and cost-accuracy tradeoff experiments across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.

title: Proactive SLO exposure triage and prioritization pipeline
preview: Implement a backend pipeline that computes a continuous SLO-exposure score per tenant+route by fusing usage, cost, and latency telemetry. The pipeline will produce prioritized triage events for the Console (and incidenting channels) that reflect both likelihood of customer-facing impact and urgency based on remaining SLO burn/budget. Goals: reduce noisy alerts, surface high-risk regressions earlier, and enable tiered escalation for Dedicated/Private customers.

title: Packaging experiments and enterprise commitments for SSO/SAML + RBAC
preview:
Summary: Several enterprise prospects are listing SSO/SAML and RBAC (fine-grained role enforcement + audit trails) as explicit contract requirements before they can sign. This ticket captures a product + pricing experiment plan to decide whether SSO/SAML and RBAC are: (A) included in an "Enterprise" tier by default, (B) sold as a separate add-on with a one-time integration/onboarding fee, or (C) sold as an included feature behind a minimum committed spend. The goal is to minimize sales friction and accelerate time-to-sign while balancing incremental revenue and engineering cost.

Context: Sales has 8 active deals where security questionnaire responses and SSO architectural diagrams are gating closure. Engineering has an estimated 6-10 week implementation path for a hardened, production-ready SAML SSO flow (IdP metadata UI, automated cert rotation, SAML tracing, SCIM integration is out of scope for initial experiment). RBAC MVP requires an admin UI for role creation, API enforcement hooks, and audit logging. Legal wants clear contract language and a standard security Q&A template. Security wants to ensure we can commit to documentation and controls before promising contractual SLAs.

Experiment plan: 1) Pricing experiment A: Offer SSO + RBAC included in a new "Redwood Enterprise" price band (list price + 12-month minimum commit). Track win rate and ARR uplift for deals > $100k ARR. 2) Pricing experiment B: Offer SSO as paid add-on (one-time integration fee between $15k-$40k depending on complexity) and RBAC as tiered per-seat/per-environment billing. 3) Operational experiment: Pilot a "Self-serve SAML enablement" path for existing paid accounts (requires validation + support checklist) vs "Sales-assisted SAML" where onboarding is a bundled professional service.

Success metrics: increase in win rate for security-sensitive deals by >= 15%, reduction in average deal cycle time by >= 10 business days, incremental ARR attributable to add-on vs baseline, engineering hours required for maintenance/support post-launch, and number of security questionnaire items we can sign confidently (target = reduce red flags by 60%).

Risks & tradeoffs: Bundling may reduce price capture for customers willing to pay; add-on model increases friction and potentially kills small-mid deals; offering self-serve SAML without hardened controls could increase support load and security incidents. Legal contracting burden increases with per-customer exceptions.

Stakeholders: PM (Priya), Product (Jordan), Engineering (runtime & infra leads), Security (Camila Ortiz), Legal (Maya Singh), Sales (regional AEs: Alex Park, Elena Ruiz).

title: Enterprise on-ramp map and quick-scope for aggregated sales asks
preview: Sales and Customer Success have been routing a high volume of non-bug enterprise feature asks as individual tickets. This work aims to create a repeatable 'on-ramp' mapping and lightweight scoping workflow that: 1) aggregates related asks from high-touch accounts, 2) produces a one-page impact & risk snapshot per bundle, and 3) outputs starter implementation tickets with 1-2 sprint 'T-shirt' estimates for Engineering. Goal is faster first-response to customers and better prioritization signals for PM/Eng.
text: Sales and Customer Success have been routing a high volume of non-bug enterprise feature asks as individual tickets. Goal is faster first-response to customers and better prioritization signals for PM/Eng.

title: Provenance-enriched evals alert stream with adaptive rate-limiting and grouping
preview: Goal: surface high-confidence quality regressions detected by automated eval runs into the Console notification stream (Slack + PagerDuty) while minimizing noise via provenance-aware grouping and adaptive rate limiting. This ticket defines the data model, dedupe grouping heuristics, routing rules, and rollout plan for the first implementation.
text: Goal: surface high-confidence quality regressions detected by automated eval runs into the Console notification stream (Slack + PagerDuty) while minimizing noise via provenance-aware grouping and adaptive rate limiting. This ticket defines the data model, dedupe grouping heuristics, routing rules, and rollout plan for the first implementation.

title: Commitment Request Decision Hub and Allocation Runbook
preview:
Problem: We currently receive a high volume of pricing and commitment requests from enterprise sales, finance, and marketplace partners that are routed inconsistently across PM, Sales Ops, and Finance. That ambiguity leads to slow decisions, duplicated work, and misallocated credits or invoice treatments. This ticket creates a single Decision Hub playbook that standardizes intake validation, prioritization heuristics, and allocation outcomes for commitment, invoicing, and marketplace credit requests.

Objective: Deliver a runnable playbook and simple tooling checklist that: 1) validates incoming request completeness, 2) maps requests to one of four outcome paths (Auto-Approve, Needs Finance Review, Needs Legal/Sales Approval, Escalate to PM), and 3) prescribes cost allocation, invoice treatment, and marketplace accounting actions.

Why this matters: Faster, deterministic handling reduces engineering time spent on ad-hoc billing patches, prevents incorrect marketplace crediting, and gives Sales transparent SLAs for commitment approvals.

Success metrics: median time-to-decision for triaged requests reduced from ~7 days to <=48 hours; >85% of low-friction requests auto-classified; fewer than 2% of approvals require retroactive billing corrections within 90 days.

title: Anchor-prompt fingerprinting and streaming-tool orchestrator smoke harness
preview: Build a compact, deterministic smoke harness that validates multi-protocol API compatibility across streaming, function/tool calling, and structured-output paths using a seeded corpus of 'anchor prompts'. The harness should exercise partial-JSON emission, tool-call handshakes, KV-cache priming scenarios, and multi-stream interleaving to catch regressions before canary deploys. Focus is on actionable failures (schema break, loss of toolcall metadata, sequence reordering) rather than exhaustive behavioral diffs.
